title: Microwave Cooking
content:
MICROWAVE COOKING

LIMITATIONS

- Most foods toughen compared to conventional cooking
- Meats dry out and lose more nutrients
- Cakes coarse-textured, over-moist, pallid
- No true browning achievable
- Limited capacity (4 servings maximum in most portable ovens)
- Only one layer of cake possible
- Requires constant attention during reheating (rotation, stirring)

WHAT WORKS

- Defrosting (with intermittent standing to prevent outside cooking before center thaws)
- Reheating (with attention to rotation/stirring)
- Boned, tender cuts of meat (rolled roasts most trouble-free)
- Covering with plastic or paper to hold steam and prevent spatter

WHAT DOESN'T WORK

- Bony or uneven cuts (requires foil covering to shield thin portions; keep foil 2 inches from walls)
- Whole hams (except reheating fully cooked)
- Moist-heat-required meats (better texture conventional)
- Rice or pasta requiring large liquid quantities
- Foods needing browning for flavor/appearance

MATERIALS FOR MICROWAVE

Safe: Glass, some plastics, ungreased white paper, some ceramics
Test: Set empty in oven; if hot after 15 seconds, discard
Dangerous: Metal or metal-trimmed dishes (causes arcing)

SAFETY

Door mechanism and switches must be flawless. Air filters clean. Oven interior unpitted. Any irregularity: service call immediately.

Combination microwave/conventional ovens and convector ovens address some limitations but still require intelligent attention for best results.
